blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days ... prior to the target date within 2021 only."
Blueprint holdout_plan: "Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date."

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics computed on 688 user-days with requires_full_7day_context=true; code restricts available_training_days to split.train_by_user for validation.

validation-conditioning-history-restrict.error_identification: Validation generation conditions Pattern/Motivation on only the training-split history (agent.available_training_days = split.train_by_user[uid]) rather than on all observed days strictly prior to each validation target date. This deviates from the Blueprint conditioning rule and can alter generated stop/time distributions.
validation-conditioning-history-restrict.root_cause_analysis: In main(), before validation rollout, the code sets ag.available_training_days = split.train_by_user.get(uid). Then ensure_pattern_upto() and _compute_motivation() use available_training_days as the sole context source. This enforces an additional constraint ("train-history only") not in the Blueprint, and can exclude legitimate pre-target observed days that lie in the validation tail but still precede later validation targets.
validation-conditioning-history-restrict.correct_approach: For each validation target day d, build context = {all observed 2021 days < d} (not future days, but not restricted to the train split). Remove the global available_training_days assignment for validation, and instead enforce no-leakage via the strict cutoff check already implemented (d < target_day). If you still want a stricter holdout, reflect it explicitly in the Blueprint/metrics design and evaluate accordingly.
validation-conditioning-history-restrict.key_insight: Don’t conflate temporal leakage prevention with “train-split-only” conditioning; enforce leakage by cutoff_day (< target) rather than by dataset split membership.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "Link POI ids to coordinates ... by matching the exact 'Category#id' string."

code_refs.symbol: load_data (adds missing POIs with lat/lon None)

trip-distance-metric-low-coord-coverage.error_identification: Trip-distance evaluation is based on only ~30% of consecutive-stop pairs having coordinates; Wasserstein distance is therefore computed on a small, biased subset and remains relatively high.
trip-distance-metric-low-coord-coverage.root_cause_analysis: load_data() logs show 3858 trajectory-only POIs are injected with lat/lon None, and the simulation can still select them (especially under exploration). Although _choose_next_poi() tries to increase coord-known share, it only hard-restricts when coord_candidates>=50 and otherwise attempts a heuristic min_coord_share, leaving many missing-coord POIs in circulation. Evaluator._trip_distances drops missing-coord legs entirely, reducing effective sample size.
trip-distance-metric-low-coord-coverage.correct_approach: Make coordinate availability a first-class constraint for distance-evaluable legs: (1) during candidate building, prefer coord-known POIs whenever the current_poi has coords and the category has any coord-known POIs; (2) when exploration selects unknown POIs, consider a two-stage: explore among coord-known first, then fallback to unknown only if no coord-known exist; (3) add a diagnostic report: per-category coord coverage and fraction of simulated stops/legs using coord-known POIs.
trip-distance-metric-low-coord-coverage.key_insight: If a metric depends on an external join (coords), ensure the generator’s sampling process matches that join’s support; otherwise evaluation becomes low-power and biased.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "daily mobility trajectories" with timing dynamics; start time distribution derived from first visit time per day.

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.025593439874026805 (good overall), but sample outputs show frequent 00:00–05:00 starts and dense overnight activity (e.g., 00:10, 00:30, 01:10).

unrealistic-overnight-and-early-day-star.error_identification: Despite low aggregate ToD divergence, many simulated days begin around midnight or very early morning, which may be unrealistic for typical daily mobility and can be an artifact of parsing/conditioning.
unrealistic-overnight-and-early-day-star.root_cause_analysis: The start-time histogram is taken directly from observed first-visit time (ResidentAgent.fit_pattern_from_training), but parsing (parse_day_record) can treat out-of-order times by clamping and can stop on overnight wraps. If the raw strings contain late-night continuation logs, the “first listed visit” may be after midnight even when conceptually part of the previous day; the code currently breaks on certain overnight patterns rather than reassigning them, and this can skew start_time_hist toward early minutes. Simulation then samples directly from this skewed histogram.
unrealistic-overnight-and-early-day-star.correct_approach: Introduce a defensible day-boundary rule for start-time estimation: when a day record contains a first timestamp in [00:00, 04:00] but later contains a timestamp >=20:00 (or indicates wrap), treat that early segment as carryover and exclude it from start_time_hist (or shift it to previous day) for pattern fitting. Additionally, clamp simulated start times to a user-specific central range (e.g., between 4th and 96th percentile of observed starts) to prevent pathological tails from dominating.
unrealistic-overnight-and-early-day-star.key_insight: Time-of-day distributions are extremely sensitive to day-boundary parsing; build explicit wrap-handling rules for “first stop” features rather than inheriting raw ordering artifacts.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "estimate P(next_category | current_category) from adjacent visits ... apply smoothing."

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence=0.02031488911651167 (good), but simulation outputs show occasional implausible jumps and repeated niche categories; smoothing currently adds +1 mass to every observed category for each c0.

category-transition-model-smoothing-over.error_identification: Transition smoothing currently distributes probability mass across all categories observed for the user, even if a category never follows a given current category; this can inject unrealistic transitions (especially for users with many distinct categories).
category-transition-model-smoothing-over.root_cause_analysis: In ResidentAgent.fit_pattern_from_training(), for each current category c0, the code iterates over all_cats and sets val = nexts.get(c1,0)+1, yielding dense transition distributions. This is a very strong smoothing scheme that can overwhelm the empirical next-category structure when all_cats is large. _choose_next_category() then mixes this transition pmf with global/user priors, potentially amplifying the dense tail.
category-transition-model-smoothing-over.correct_approach: Change smoothing to be sparse and conditional: only smooth over (a) categories observed as successors of c0 plus (b) a small backoff set (e.g., top-N user categories or global time-profile categories for the current ToD bin). Use a smaller additive alpha (e.g., 0.1) and/or interpolation with a backoff distribution rather than making every row dense.
category-transition-model-smoothing-over.key_insight: Additive smoothing over the full category vocabulary turns personalized Markov rows into near-uniform noise; smooth over a principled backoff set and interpolate instead of densifying.

blueprint_refs: Blueprint: "awareness boost ... decays over time ... within the same or next day."

evidence.metrics: No direct failure; calibration does reset awareness, but rollout itself does not enforce a hard reset and relies on external callers.

social-influence-state-can-leak-across-r.error_identification: Awareness is intended to be short-lived; correctness currently depends on callers remembering to reset before each rollout. This is fragile and can produce inconsistent results if rollout() is used elsewhere.
social-influence-state-can-leak-across-r.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator.rollout() updates and decays agent.category_awareness but does not internally reset at the start; resets happen in BaseCalibrator.fit(), RandomSearchCalibrator.fit(), and main() validation setup. Any future reuse of rollout() without these resets will carry state across experiments/days beyond the intended mechanism.
social-influence-state-can-leak-across-r.correct_approach: Add an optional argument to rollout() like reset_awareness: bool = True, and when true call reset_all_awareness(self.agents) at rollout start. Keep the external resets too, but make rollout safe-by-default.
social-influence-state-can-leak-across-r.key_insight: Stateful social effects should be reset or scoped within the function that simulates them; don’t rely on external orchestration for correctness.
